data.title: KBE: Best-In-Class Structure, But Cyclical Risk Comes With The Territory
data.description:
Even though the State Street SPDR S&P Bank ETF (KBE) was born as a solution to represent the US banking segment, the nature of the reference index leads it to always maintain a certain imbalance toward the regional banking segment. Which gives KBE a “softened” small cap factor that makes the ETF very fascinating and certainly distinctive compared to more widely used solutions like XLF or KRE. But in what way is KBE built, what kind of exposure does it bring in a portfolio? and who is it aimed at? All very valid questions. But let’s go step by step.

Introduction to the State Street SPDR S&P Bank ETF

KBE is an ETF managed by SSGA Funds Management, Inc. in a completely systematic manner and without discretionary intervention, launched on November 8, 2005. With an AUM of ~$1.4 bn, the fund replicates the total return of the S&P Banks Select Industry index, which represents the US banking segment (money center + regional banks). The method used by KBE is Modified Equal Weight, which is adjusted through quarterly rebalancing.

The expense ratio is 0.35%, to which a negligible bid/ask spread of 0.02% is added, not to mention a low turnover (22%). Overall, however, this is covered by a dividend yield of 2.60%.

What Does KBE Do?

It makes sense to start from the index inclusion criteria: this selects solutions with a float-adjusted market cap over $2B and a liquidity coverage ratios over 100%. First consideration: even though it selects around 100 components, the index is effectively drawing from a very large pool of stocks. At this point, if an equal weight inclusion method is also added, it is natural that there will be direct impacts on asset allocation: this in fact reduces the dominance of money center banks and amplifies the weight of regional banks.

In fact, with over 74% in regional banks, we can say that KBE is a solution primarily oriented toward portfolio coverage of the US regional banking segment rather than a global financial exposure as one might have erroneously inferred from the name. This makes KBE highly sensitive to the risk and return drivers of the regional banking segment, and especially small cap.

Who Is KBE For?

As a result, by exclusion, KBE will not be suitable for those looking for a diversified, global exposure to the financials segment. Rather, it is aimed at those seeking a US-centric solution, skewed toward the regional banking segment. And this has distinctive consequences in portfolio terms. First of all, on momentum:

The expansion and contraction cycles of the regional banking segment more closely follow the dynamics of the small cap value market, rather than the S&P 500. In this sense, the parallel with my pick from a small cap perspective, AVUV, is interesting.

Likewise, they share the same risk, therefore volatility. In fact, the volatility of KBE, even though financials are often associated with a defensive sector, is higher than that of the S&P 500. Precisely because its nature leads it to be more comparable to broader benchmarks such as the Russell 2000.

This makes it a solution poorly suited for those seeking defensive assets: an annualized volatility of 25% brings portfolio volatility, certainly not protection.

For this reason, KBE could be proposed as a tactical or satellite portfolio solution rather than a core holding or a full representation of the financials segment component within a diversified portfolio.

How Is KBE Built?

103 well-distributed stocks (only 11.46% in the top 10) with an average market cap of ~$30 bn, which confirms the real mid-cap bias typical of the regional banking segment.

At this point, the confirmation lies precisely in the sector distribution, which can be outlined as follows, confirming over 74% in regional banks, and just over 8% in commercial and residential mortgage finance.

The equal weight nature emerges clearly here: stocks such as JPM, Citi, Wells are performance stabilizers that are practically irrelevant because they weigh the same as a regional bank.

And we also find confirmation in valuations: the forward P/E is low, at 11.88x; the reason should be sought in the estimated EPS growth, here equal to 15.04%, with a P/B. These are valuations clearly lower at first glance than both the S&P 500 and more widely recognized ETFs such as XLF (respectively with P/E of 27x and 18x). At the same time, it is necessary to pay attention to a series of elements:

  • The expected EPS is very cyclical, it assumes credit stability, no recession, and normalization of funding costs.
  • Relative valuations have a historical discount, but one that is consistent with a highly cyclical sector such as small cap value, and not comparable in absolute terms with the S&P 500 and XLF.

Risks

On the one hand, the equal weight method brings diversification; on the other, at least in the financial sector, it also leads to a neutralization of quality. This is because it results in a flattening of the difference between a solid balance sheet and a fragile one, which makes the ETF more sensitive to credit dynamics from an overall perspective.

A structure composed of over 80% Regional Banks and Mortgage Finance entails high sensitivity to macro drivers that are often difficult to control and/or predict, such as real rates, the yield curve, commercial real estate trends, and credit quality. And today, with credit conditions remaining fairly tight, with open adjusted spreads at multi-year lows, this is not an element to forget.

Peer comparison

At this point, naturally, the peer table should be oriented toward solutions exposed at least predominantly to the regional banking sector. In this sense, here is a fairly exhaustive peer table of the segment.

Simply extending performance to 5Y, an outperformance of KBE can be observed, even though, looking a bit further back, it is clear that this has not always been the case. During the 2022 dump, with the tightening of credit conditions, KBE was also one of the worst performers. This confirms its relatively volatile nature and sensitivity to monetary policy expectations. It should not be forgotten that, albeit in a limited and marginal way, KBE also includes solutions that are extraneous to the regional bank segment, an element that, for example, distinguishes it from other more niche solutions such as KRE. This is why returns and volatility often diverge.

That said, it still remains the solution with the most advantageous expense ratio together with KRE and KBWR, and the one with the highest AUM among the selected peers together with KRE.

Pros & Cons:

KBE has distinctive elements that work in its favor, which can be summarized as follows:

  1. An equal-weight structure, which entails better management of idiosyncratic risk. Here, all stocks account for ~1–1.35%.
  2. Compressed valuation compared to US equity and broad financials, which could signal a negative asymmetry that is already partially priced in.
  3. A competitive expense-to-distribution ratio, especially thanks to the presence of a positive dividend yield.

And naturally, KBE is not without flaws:

  1. Strong factor concentration in regional banks
  2. Which gives it negative sensitivity to the rate-cutting cycle
  3. And the absence of internal stabilizers within the portfolio: stocks such as JPM, Citi, and Wells Fargo weigh the same as a regional bank and do not provide an anti-cyclical function.

This article answers these three main questions about KBE:

  • Does KBE have international holdings or is it strictly domestic?
  • Is KBE subjected to cyclical risks as a result of investing only in the banking sector?
  • What type of investor is KBE best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Nvidia: 2026 Is Likely To Be A Year Of Consolidation (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Nvidia Faces A Mixed Set Of Catalysts

I last covered Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) on Nov 13 with an article titled "Nvidia Q3 Earnings Preview: Robust Margins Met With A China Problem.” That article served as a preview of its Q3 earnings report (ER, released on Nov 19) and rated the stock as a Hold. Since then, a mixed set of new catalysts have surfaced around this leading chipmaker.

In the rest of this article, I elaborate my thoughts on 3 developments since the ER: the new development with its China market, the rise of custom silicon, and also the latest technical trading patterns. These developments have led me to the year of 2026 as a year of consolidation for the stock. As the AI sector continues to transition from its initial explosive growth phase into a period of infrastructure maturity in 2026, Nvidia faces a mixed set of challenges and opportunities due to these developments from uncertainties of global trade and intensified competition from hyperscalers.

Let me start with the development of its China market. The latest development on this front keeps presenting a complex outlook for Nvidia in 2026. For readers unfamiliar with the background, President Trump approved the export of Nvidia's H200 GPUs to China earlier this month. More specifics are quoted below:

Seeking Alpha News: Nvidia (NVDA) has plans in place to start shipping its H200 GPUs to China by the middle of February, assuming government approval… Assuming government approval, Nvidia will add production capacity, with orders coming from the added production in the second quarter. President Trump said earlier this month that he had approved the sale of Nvidia's H200 GPUs, which are less powerful than its Blackwell line, to China, for a 25% fee.

This development seems positive at first glance. However, after thinking about it more, I am not certain A) if it is all positive, and B) even if it is, how much impact it eventually would have on its bottom line. To start, based on its Q3 financials (see the pie chart below), NVDA’s China sales only account for a relatively small portion of its total revenue (about 13%). I expect the impact on the bottom line to be even smaller due to the 25% fee and other considerations. The H200 GPUs are less advanced and powerful than its Blackwell line, and I thus expected lower margins on these exports. Furthermore, the demand in China is uncertainty too in my view. As detailed in my last article, Chinese domestic AI chips are advancing at a good pace, and many end users there might be reluctant (or discouraged by the central government) to use NVDA chips.

To further compound the uncertainty, internal chip development by major U.S. customers like Alphabet/Google (GOOGL) is eroding Nvidia’s pricing power too, as detailed next.

Nvidia: More Threatened By Google Than Chip Peers

The latest development in the AI ecosystem has led me to form the view is Nvidia is more likely to be threatened by hyperscalers like Google (and Amazon (AMZN), to a secondary degree) than by chip peers like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). In particular, Google has recently released its new Gemini 3 model (shortly after my last NVDA analysis). The market responses on the model, both from institutional analysts and also users, have been quite positive. Gemini 3 marks a hardware milestone in my view for Google custom silicon effort. The model is trained on Google’s own AI chips known as TPUs (Tensor Processing Units). After several generations, these chips have evolved sufficiently to be a meaningful threat to Nvidia's GPUs for AI applications, in my view. The Gemini 3 model was trained with a combination of the 5th and 6th generations(v5e and the v6). I won’t be surprised to see the next generation (TPUv7) to find wide adoption soon too. As a reflection, key customers like Meta Platforms (META) and Anthropic was reported to be in talks with Google to buy these TPUs for their AI applications.

Based on the specifications available (see an example below), my assessment is that the total cost of ownership (including upfront purchasing cost and also ongoing power cost) for Google’s TPUv7 would be about 40% lower than Nvidia’s GB200 chips. Besides Google, other hyperscalers like Amazon have also made considerable progress in recent years with products specifically targeting machine learning (ML, such as AMZN’s Trainium chips) and high-performance storage products. With the rise of these products, I don’t expect Nvidia to be the only viable option for large-scale AI inference, which could both impact its sales growth and also its profit margins.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

These above uncertainties are also reflected in the stock’s recent technical trading patterns. As seen in the chart below, the share price has been oscillating around its 20-day moving average (MA) since August, which has flattened itself and lost its upward momentum. More recently, since early November, the chart has been forming a downward wedging pattern between a peak of ~$210 and a support near $170. Furthermore, the bottom panel highlights a 14-day Relative Strength Index (RSI) of 52, a quite neutral reflection of a close balance between buying and selling pressure.

The last technical signal I want to draw your attention to is the price-volume bar highlighted by the blue box. This is the trading window that has attracted the large trading volumes in the past 6 months. NVDA’s current stock price is square within this range of around $175 to $185. I anticipate it to take a while for the large number of investors participating in this range to be replaced by, or converted to, with another group of investors with a renewed bullish view, especially amid the fundamental uncertainties mentioned above.

Finally, in terms of valuation, the stock is also on the expensive side, both in absolute terms and relative to close peers. As an example, compared to the mag 7 group (see the next table), NVDA’s current FWD P/E of 39.17x is only second to TSLA.

To close, this NVDA follow-up was mainly motivated by three new developments since my last writing: its China market, internal silicon developments from hyperscalers, and also the latest technical trading indicators. Combined with elevated valuation risks, these catalysts suggest neutral outlook to me and I see large odds for the stocks prices to be range bound in 2026.

data.title: Nebius: A Gift That Will Keep On Giving
data.description:
This summer we called for a public Buy rating on Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) in the mid-$40s. There was a major run in shares the next few months. More recently, there has been corrective behavior, and the further the shares dipped under $100, the more we got behind it once again. Tech has rebounded some, and NBIS is off recent lows.

On the surface, the stock looks expensive, but when you factor in the ongoing growth, major company AI-related spending, and the investments being made to build out the AI infrastructure, NBIS shares are attractive in the long run. With the holidays upon us, consider stuffing your stocking with a few shares of NBIS on the big dips. It will be a gift that keeps on giving. Does this mean it goes straight up? Absolutely not. Will shares be higher one year from now? Barring a market meltdown, the answer is yes. Folks, we want to be clear. We maintain the position that Nebius is a long-term winner.

Growth driven by explosive revenue growth and increased ARR

The big-time growth is being driven by big-time expansion in revenue, as well as significant expansion in recurring revenue (aka its ARR). As we move into 2026, we are on the watch for the making of new deals and bringing in new customers. Given the huge investments being made to expand the company and meet customer needs, the company has been burning through a lot of cash. This burning of cash the last handful of quarters is in direct relation to the massive focus on building out AI infrastructure. It reflects the massive capex spending big players are making. And a number of companies are beneficiaries, including Nebius.

Of course, this catalyst is also a risk. If capex spending comes down meaningfully on AI spend, Nebius and other competitors will feel the pinch. But for now, it's bulls on parade. While losing money to expand rapidly and meet customer needs, Nebius is in a great position to help meet the needs for large-scale GPUs and cloud platforms. Nebius has, of course, responded to the demand by raising capital to fund ongoing projects. We know it has some major deals out there with big Mag-7 names, like Meta (META). Make no mistake, the hypergrowth is set to continue in 2026.

Expanding key metrics cannot be ignored

When the company reported Q3 earnings, revenue surged to $146.1 million, a massive increase from the $105 million in the sequential Q2 alone. This was also up from $55 million in Q1 of 2026, an eye-popping increase of 355% year-over-year. While that immense pace of revenue growth cannot last due to the law of large numbers, the growth in absolute dollars on the top line is set to continue. It all stems from spending cash on its investments in the core AI infrastructure business. The all-important ARR figure? It had been ramping toward a key $1 billion milestone this year. By the end of next year, that ARR is set to be $7-$9 billion with contracted power to hit 2.5GW. Yes, heavy losses are to continue, but margin power and EBITDA are improving significantly.

So, as you can expect, as revenues surge, so will the cost of revenue. As we saw recently, those costs ballooned $42.9 million from $9.9 million last year and were up $12.8 million from the sequential quarter. All cost lines are rising, which should not be a surprise. You have to spend money to make money, as they say. While selling expenses, depreciation, and R&D costs have all surged, as of now, as a percentage of revenues, all of these expenses are actually lower. As the company builds out to reach its target $7-$9 billion in ARR by this time next year, expect operating expenses to increase dramatically.

Like many others in this space, share-based compensation is also a line to watch. It is not just an expense but is dilutive. This compensation keeps talent in-house and gives them "skin in the game." It is not going away any time soon.

Losing money, for how long?

Now, we are out here suggesting you stuff your stockings with some shares. Should you load up all at once? No, but on the recent pullback we saw in tech, that was a good time to add. Much like a family starts its holiday shopping well in advance, so should you, picking up sales when they arise. We just had one. We may or may not have a Santa rally this week and next, but pick your posts. We fully acknowledge that investing here is investing in the future. And that is a bet. As mentioned, if AI spending comes down in the future, Nebius and competitors will take a hit. As it stands now, despite the huge revenue growth, as expenses ramp up for the company, Nebius still loses big money. The question is how long will that continue? At least a few more quarters to be sure.

Each time capital is raised, it is quickly spent on building out capacity and retaining talent. That comes too with being diluted. That is a fair, bearish point. But EBITDA is trending in the right direction. Adjusted EBITDA was still a loss in Q3, but just $5.2 million, much better than the $45.9 million last year. However, on the bottom line as adjusted, losses are mounting. Year-over-year losses ballooned 153% to $100.4 million compared to last year. Sequentially, about another additional $10 million was lost. However, as more and more deals are put into place, capacity expansion expenses normalize some, revenues come in from the partnerships, and revenue growth continues with improving margins, earnings positivity, and positive cash flow are in the cards. Will that be in 2026? Unlikely. However, 2027 could be where the tide turns.

Early estimates suggest another year of losses in 2026, but 2027, for now, looks to swing positive. We, of course, need to get updates and guidance from the company to better understand progress on meeting revenue targets, get a feel for the trend in expense growth, and understand cash burn, but the early outlook now is for inflection in the bottom line in 2027.

Do earnings matter? Of course, yes. But at this stage of the game, it is still about ramping revenue and capacity. As these are achieved, the Street will then shift to caring more about the bottom line. For now, shares look expensive, but considering where the company can potentially be in 2-3 years, the big-time pullbacks are a bargain to consider here. It takes time before the massive spending can normalize. So, expect ongoing net losses for the next year, but watch for inflection. We think having some exposure to this hypergrowth on weakness is worth the investment. Take advantage of the holiday sale on shares.

data.title: U.S. GDP Delivers Another Christmas Surprise While Canadian Economy Contracts - Market Reactions
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Santa came with another positive surprise for the US, but despite the snow, there wasn't any Christmas miracle for Canada.

The US GDP release was indeed a strong hit, coming in at an annualized 4.3% (with the price index for Q3 coming in at 3.7% vs. 2.7% expected).

Stellar numbers on the surface, but the strong data may raise a few questions.

A significant portion of this growth could be driven by a sharp decrease in imports, which mathematically boosts the headline GDP figure even if domestic demand isn't accelerating at the same pace.

It’s a classic accounting quirk that could mask underlying softness - or at least, make the economy look hotter than the labor market suggests.

Industrial production offered a glimmer of stabilization, confirming your figures with a -0.1% read for October and a +0.2% rebound in November, still totalling for a two-month (slight) increase.

We will likely have to wait for the final yearly publications to see if this is a genuine rise.

Yet, the S&P 500 doesn't seem to care, shaking off an open gap-down but overall seesawing action for stocks.

Canada's cutting cycle - slashing rates from 5.50% to 2.25% since mid 2024 -hasn't yet sparked a revival, confirmed by the disappointing -0.3% GDP print for October.

But as GDP is a lagging data and employment showed decent upside, the upbeat data should reflect in the GDP only later next year.

Let's explore reactions to the data with our market overview.

Volatility should compress as we approach the rest of the day and the end of the year.

Of course, keep an eye on any geopolitical disruptions that could be the only real factor for movement, particularly if nobody is there to absorb liquidity shocks. But as long as nothing happens, expect a slow, sideways grind for the next few weeks.

Safe trades, and merry Christmas!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia Is Not A Bubble, Here's Why
data.description:
As I said several times before, the first time I invested in Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) was in late 2022. Since then, NVDA has been one of my favorite businesses to follow and continue investing. Yes, I invested many times since then, even this year, so I can state right from the get-go that I don't consider NVDA overvalued.

NVDA's performance in 2025 was very strong and I believe it proved its capabilities to navigate around geopolitical shifts and tensions (I've explained that in detail in this article, so feel free to take a look if you'd like). Since the tariff-related dip, NVDA's stock price has increased by over 88% - I can see it's quite stabilized at current levels ranging from $170 to $200 for a couple of months now.

Since the last time I covered NVDA, we received several important insights from both the Company and other market participants. I've observed increasing concerns about the "AI bubble" and "Nvidia's overvaluation." For instance, Reuters reported yesterday that many investors are betting on China instead of the U.S. in terms of AI technology - all of that is naturally connected to the fear of a "bubble":

Global investors turn to Chinese AI as Wall Street fears bubble.

(...)

Global investors are increasing their wagers on Chinese artificial intelligence companies, betting on the next DeepSeek and seeking to diversify, with concerns growing about a speculative bubble in the sector on Wall Street.

So... I decided to prepare a follow-up coverage to show these insights I consider some of the key factors of NVDA's further growth and sector domination. But first things first - I'd like to address the so-called "AI bubble" first, so that Nvidia's investors (including me) can rest assured.

AI is not a bubble, and neither is Nvidia - here's why

I understand investors are concerned about the risk of forming a bubble. It's a healthy approach, we should all be skeptical - that's how we remain reasonable with our investing decisions. I have no doubts that there are investors who genuinely fear that the technological market reached a bubble - I often see comparisons to a dot-com bubble:

The immense amount of investment being poured into AI is an undisputed fact, companies and investors are piling billions into the ecosystem, driving valuations to unseen heights. Comparisons between today’s artificial intelligence boom and the dot-com bubble of the late 1990s and early 2000s are becoming increasingly common. Some commentators even claim the US economy is hinging on this bubble and is bound to crash when it pops. In both cases, we see the rise of a transformative technology, a rapid surge in company valuations, and soaring expectations.

But... there's one crucial thing such commentators are missing - earnings potential. We can easily analyze it through valuation multiples. Why do they matter? While valuation is an important aspect of making investment decisions, the key is to review valuation relative to income potential. In simpler words - I always try to answer the following question: how many dollars do I have to pay for one dollar of earnings that a given business generates?

The chart above shows why investors are afraid that Nvidia is just another bubble as they compare the dynamic valuation growth to the symbol of a dot-com bubble - Cisco (CSCO). Now, NVDA's valuation is even higher than presented in the chart. However, the picture looks differently when we account for the income potential via reviewing forward-looking P/E multiple, doesn't it?

At the top of the bubble Cisco climbed as high as ~100x 2-year forward-looking P/E ratio - I think that if the comparison between the current market levels ("AI bubble") and the dot-com bubble was solid, multiples of the most relevant players should be at least comparable. Is that the case?

I wanted to show you a summary of key hardware providers that facilitate the ongoing AI revolution (including NVDA) - the chart above serves this purpose. As you can see, none of these businesses trades at nearly 100x multiples and on top of that, it's not like NVDA's multiples are the highest. There's even a case to be made that some of them are heavily undervalued - I think Micron is a perfect example of that situation, as I explained in my latest coverage (here's the link).

I think the same applies to businesses playing at the front-line of the AI revolution (check the chart above) - clients of businesses I mentioned earlier that invest heavily in data center infrastructure to facilitate their cloud and computing capabilities.

My verdict? Nvidia is not a bubble, it's not overvalued, and I still see its significant potential to not only grow into its valuation further but to keep increasing it as it reaches next milestones. In fact... as I mentioned earlier, I have a few insights to share with you.

Three updates making the bull case stronger

#1 Nvidia pushes its open-source AI offering further

Is Nvidia a hardware or a software business? Naturally, it's mainly recognized as a hardware business, but I like to keep in mind that NVDA's software (mainly CUDA) is as crucial as every other aspect of its business. As Reuters mentioned:

Its proprietary CUDA software, a standard among most developers, is a major selling point for its chips, making software key to maintaining its dominance in the AI industry.

Also, it offers a handful of open-source models that other businesses can use to build their own products - it keeps developing the range of these models with new addition announced last week (Nemotron) and some announced to be released in 2026:

Nvidia is primarily known for providing chips that firms such as OpenAI use to train their closed-source models and charge money for them. But it also offers a slew of its own models for everything from physics simulations to self-driving vehicles as open-source software that can be used by researchers or by other companies, with firms such as Palantir Technologies weaving Nvidia's model into their products.

Nvidia on Monday revealed the third generation of its "Nemotron" large-language models aimed at writing, coding and other tasks. The smallest of the models, called Nemotron 3 Nano, was being released Monday, with two other, larger versions coming in the first half of 2026.

Some of you probably know this already - I'm an M&A advisor, so I always like to see some deals, as they are especially interesting to me given my day-to-day occupation. That's why Nvidia surprised me positively with SchedMD acquisition that further strengthens its open-source offer:

NVIDIA today announced it has acquired SchedMD — the leading developer of Slurm, an open-source workload management system for high-performance computing (HPC) and AI — to help strengthen the open-source software ecosystem and drive AI innovation for researchers, developers and enterprises.

(...)

Slurm, which is supported on the latest NVIDIA hardware, is also part of the critical infrastructure needed for generative AI, used by foundation model developers and AI builders to manage model training and inference needs.

#2 The Chinese market will reopen and strengthen soon

Ach, the export to China... it was quite a ride wasn't it? A lot happened on that front in 2025, but I was happy to read just yesterday that Nvidia wants to start shipping its H200 chips to China in just two months from now (around the half of February 2026). Reuters reported that:

The U.S. chipmaker plans to fulfil initial orders from existing stock, with shipments expected to total 5,000 to 10,000 chip modules - equivalent to about 40,000 to 80,000 H200 AI chips, the first and second sources said.

How's the demand? I think it'll be strong. In fact, it's expected to be strong enough to incline Nvidia to increase its output of H200 (while stating clearly that entering China again won't impact the supply to U.S. clients):

Nvidia (NVDA.O), opens new tab has told Chinese clients it is evaluating adding production capacity for its powerful H200 AI chips after orders exceeded its current output level, according to two sources briefed on the matter.

The move comes after U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday the U.S. government would allow Nvidia to export H200 processors, its second-fastest AI chips, to China and collect a 25% fee on such sales.

(...)

"We are managing our supply chain to ensure that licensed sales of the H200 to authorized customers in China will have no impact on our ability to supply customers in the United States," an Nvidia spokesperson said in a statement to Reuters after the story was published.

Major Chinese companies including Alibaba (9988.HK), opens new tab and ByteDance have already reached out to Nvidia this week about purchasing the H200 and are keen to place large orders, Reuters reported on Wednesday.

#3 Stellar product roadmap ahead with Rubin going to market in 2026

Nvidia's Executive VP & CFO (Colette Kress) has recently presented at UBS Global Technology and AI Conference 2025 - I was happy to read the transcript of that conference, as Colette Kress gave us some solid updates on NVDA's market outlook and product progress. What do I have in mind?

Firstly, Nvidia is looking at a $3T to $4T opportunity in the broad data center market by the end of 2030 (I understand it's attributable to several factors, incl. changes of chips from previous architectures to new ones, expanding computing demand, as well as progressing transition from CPUs to GPUs).

So when we think about our outlook of by the end of the decade, $3 trillion to $4 trillion worth of AI or just total data center infrastructure (...)

How will NVDA capture as much of the market as it can? Just take a look at the product roadmap it has ahead of itself - after a smooth and successful introduction of Blackwell Nvidia is working hard on the Rubin architecture with Feynman expected for 2028. Vera Rubin should go to market in the second half of 2026:

We're very pleased both with what occurred with Ultra. People come in and say, it was seamless, and that's what we wanted. So a seamless transition, very, very helpful for many in the new models that they were creating. And I think you're going to see an X factor increase also with performance as we think about Vera Rubin. So it's right around the corner for the second half of next year. We're very excited for it.

My conclusion? Nvidia is a "Strong Buy"

I established a few things in this article. I think the most important aspect is showing the difference between the dot-com bubble and the current stage of the market - Nvidia is not a bubble in my opinion and its valuation multiples (as well as multiples of other key market players) confirm that. While NVDA is not "cheap," buying at ~39x forward-looking earnings can still prove profitable for investors. Please keep in mind that Nvidia traded at even higher levels throughout the last couple of years and buying it worked pretty well. How's that possible? Growth. Nvidia is one of the most capable businesses in terms of growing into its valuation.

Regarding the insights I shared, I believe each of them supports Nvidia's growth story. Promising acquisitions, expanding open-source offering, strong progress on new architectures, and getting the Chinese market up and running again - I see these news as highly positive and believe they make the bull case even stronger (not to mention high double-digit revenue growth recording each quarter).

Despite a stabilized stock price level recorded during the last couple of months, I think investors can still expect high double-digit total returns as Nvidia keeps growing its scale and bottom-line, as well as cementing the leadership position. That's why NVDA remains a Strong Buy to me.

data.title: The Expanding Frontier Of Digital Infrastructure
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

As 2025 has unfolded, the conversation around digital infrastructure has taken on the scale and energy once reserved for the early internet. Data centers and compute capacity have become the new oilfields. They are the massive, capital-intensive assets powering artificial intelligence, cloud-based application economies and an emerging class of autonomous digital systems. OpenAI (OPENAI), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), Oracle (ORCL) and others are announcing multi-gigawatt buildouts as if they were announcing the next great hydroelectric projects of the 20th century.1 Each new deployment represents not just racks of graphics processing unites (GPUs) but an investment in national competitiveness, industrial policy and the physical substrate of intelligence itself. Compute has become a geopolitical resource.

Yet, focusing exclusively on data centers risks mistaking the visible tip for a much larger and more intricate iceberg. The next generation of digital infrastructure isn't confined to compute; it's the full nervous system of the digital world. Fiber routes, subsea cables, edge interconnects, satellite constellations and power distribution networks are all being quietly re-engineered to support AI's insatiable hunger for bandwidth and resilience. The demand for ultra-low-latency data transmission, synchronized compute across continents and renewable power at the edge is forcing a rethinking of how digital systems physically exist in space. In short, the entire internet's plumbing is being rebuilt in real time.

And here lies the opportunity: the world is converging on an era where compute, connectivity and energy form a single strategic triad. The firms that recognize this convergence, whether they're hyperscalers, utilities or specialized real estate investment trusts (REITs), are not merely building infrastructure; they are defining the industrial logic of the AI century. The next digital superpowers may not be the companies that own the best models, but those that control the layers of infrastructure enabling intelligence to move, think and power itself globally. The story of data centers is just beginning, but the true narrative of digital infrastructure is much bigger, and much more exciting, than most investors yet realize.

The WisdomTree New Economy Real Estate Framework

The WisdomTree New Economy Real Estate Fund (WTRE) is designed to capture the transformation of real estate from a traditional, income-focused sector into a core enabler of modern technology. Its foundation is the WisdomTree New Economy Real Estate Index, a rules-based benchmark that selects companies at the intersection of property and digital infrastructure. Each candidate company is evaluated through four thematic pillars: thematic relevance, market positioning, scalability and potential, and innovation. Firms must derive at least 50% of their revenue, profit or assets from activities tied to digital and industrial economy infrastructure, such as data centers, telecom towers, life sciences labs and modern logistics, or from emerging next-generation digital assets like blockchain hosting companies and high-performance compute facilities.

The Index intentionally balances stability with innovation, allocating 90% of its weight to established "Digital and Industrial Economy Infrastructure" and 10% to "Next-Generation Digital Infrastructure." This ensures exposure not only to large-cap leaders like Equinix,2 American Tower3 and Prologis,4 which anchor the ecosystem through data center and connectivity assets, but also to frontier companies such as Core Scientific,5 Applied Digital6 and Cipher Mining,7 which represent the more cutting edge infrastructure of the AI and blockchain economy. Each constituent is weighted by free float adjusted market cap, and the Index is reconstituted semi-annually to maintain alignment with evolving technology and market dynamics. In practice, this approach means WTRE systematically identifies the real estate assets most critical to megatrends, AI compute, global connectivity, digital logistics and biotechnology, ensuring that companies like IREN8 or AST SpaceMobile9 appear in the portfolio not by coincidence, but by design.

The Engines behind 2025's Next-Gen Infrastructure Rally

The performance story of 2025 isn't being written by the usual suspects in mega cap tech. Instead, it's being driven by a cohort of next-generation infrastructure companies that most investors had barely heard of two years ago. Take Iris Energy (IREN): originally pigeonholed as a Bitcoin miner, it's rapidly evolving into one of the most efficient AI compute operators on the planet. IREN's multi-gigawatt expansion plans in North America are strategically co-located with renewable energy assets, turning stranded hydro and wind capacity into AI horsepower.10 It has become a poster child for the convergence of digital assets, clean energy and high-performance compute, a story that feels as much about industrial reinvention as it does about technology.

Applied Digital (APLD) and Cipher Mining (CIFR) are two others that embody this reinvention. Both began life in blockchain infrastructure but have transformed their data centers into multi-purpose compute campuses, striking deals with AI firms hungry for power and space. Applied Digital's expansion in Texas, paired with a power purchasing strategy that rivals the sophistication of utilities, has positioned it as a stealth beneficiary of the AI boom. Cipher, meanwhile, has leaned on its low-cost energy model and modular design to deploy new capacity at a breathtaking pace. Its edge isn't just technical; it's logistical.11 These companies show that the infrastructure winners of this decade are often those who understood energy economics first, and compute economics second.

And then there's AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), pushing the frontier literally off-planet. Its vision, bringing direct-to-device broadband connectivity from space, is no longer a moonshot. With successful satellite tests and commercial partnerships announced in 2025, AST is turning what once seemed speculative into a viable complement to terrestrial fiber networks.12

Together, these firms are defining a new investment category that sits between technology and infrastructure, between speculative and indispensable. The catalysts driving them—AI demand, renewable integration, low-latency communications—are compounding rather than fading. Yet with valuations running hot and capital costs still high, investors face the dual challenge of distinguishing between sustained transformation and temporary exuberance. Either way, it's clear: the "pipes and power" of the AI era are being built now, and the companies behind them are no longer operating in the shadows.

Performance within the Morningstar Global Real Estate Peer Group

The Morningstar Global Real Estate category is designed to capture strategies investing across the global listed property universe, typically including developers, landlords and REITs with commercial, residential, retail and diversified holdings.13

WTRE stands out precisely because it breaks from a more traditional real estate exposure template. Since April 21, 2022, the Fund transitioned from tracking the WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Real Estate Index to the CenterSquare New Economy Real Estate Index, redefining its scope from conventional property exposure to the infrastructure powering the digital economy. The subsequent shift, on April 10, 2025, where WTRE began to track the total return performance, before fees, of the WisdomTree New Economy Real Estate Index, tilted exposures even further in this direction. This overall shift toward technology and innovation coincided with the rapid acceleration of AI, cloud and blockchain adoption, real estate segments far removed from traditional office or retail properties.

Over the longer horizons, the legacy exposure to non-U.S., traditional real estate assets shows in the numbers. For example, an annualized 2.85% return over 10 years ranks the Fund in the bottom decile of the peer group. Those figures reflected the structural headwinds faced by legacy real estate strategies during a period of rising rates, declining office demand and regional concentration outside the U.S., a reminder that the "old economy" REIT universe struggled to deliver real returns in a world shifting toward digital infrastructure.

Since the tilt toward technology, however, WTRE's performance has been strikingly different. Its one-year return of 13.79% and three-year annualized return of 14.76% each rank the strategy fourth among more than 150 managers and ahead of 98% of peers for the respective periods. This underscores how the strategy's thematic focus has aligned with the strongest recent secular tailwinds in real estate. The exposure to data centers, digital logistics and next-gen infrastructure has transformed WTRE from a yield-driven Fund into a growth-oriented real asset strategy. It's a powerful illustration of how redefining "real estate" to include the physical backbone of the AI and cloud economy can turn a lagging category participant into one of its top performers. The pivot wasn't cosmetic, it was structural, positioning WTRE as an early leader in what may become the dominant form of real estate investing in the coming decade.

We see the full peer group rankings in figure 2, while we see the standardized performance as of September 30, 2025, in figure 1.

Figure 1: Standardized Performance

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically data from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed 10/13/25, but showing returns for the period ended 9/30/25. WTRE's investment objective, strategy and policy changed effective 10/10/25. Prior to that date, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the CenterSquare New Economy Real Estate Index. On 10/21/22, WTRE shifted from tracking the total return performance of the WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Real Estate Index to the CenterSquare New Economy Real Estate Index. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Figure 2: Rankings within the Morningstar Global Real Estate Category

Source: Morningstar Direct. The longest available period is 7/1/07–9/20/25, based on 7/1/07 being the closest next month start relative to WTRE's inception on 6/5/07. WTRE's investment objective, strategy and policy changed effective 10/10/25. Prior to that date, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the CenterSquare New Economy Real Estate Index. On 4/21/24, WTRE shifted from tracking the total return performance of the WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Real Estate Index to the CenterSquare New Economy Real Estate Index. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Conclusion: Building the Physical Internet for the Age of Intelligence

The headlines about OpenAI's latest infrastructure ambitions for multiple gigawatts of new data center capacity over the next several years should stop readers in their tracks. A gigawatt isn't an abstract number; it's roughly the continuous power consumption of 750,000 U.S. homes. To bring even a single gigawatt of AI compute online, it takes not only racks of GPUs but also transmission lines, substations, cooling systems, fiber links and interconnection hubs, all working in unison. Every incremental watt of compute capacity ripples through a vast physical network of assets, from hyperscale campuses to modular edge deployments, from energy storage systems to network backbones orbiting the Earth. The scale of what's being built today rivals the great infrastructure expansions of the 20th century - it's the Hoover Dam era for digital civilization.

Despite this surge in construction, the system remains fully utilized. As of late 2025, there are no "dark GPUs" sitting idle. Every chip brought online finds work, training models, running inference or powering new generative applications.14 This real-world utilization data dispels the notion of an AI overbuild; demand continues to outrun supply in nearly every geography. The bottleneck isn't investor enthusiasm, it's available energy, land and fiber. That's why the conversation around AI infrastructure cannot stop at Nvidia (NVDA) or AMD (AMD). Those firms are designing the brains of the system, but the body, the connective tissue that moves data, power and signal, is where the compounding investment opportunity lies.

WTRE's framework is built for this reality. The rise of data center REITs, Bitcoin miners repurposing capacity for AI workloads, low earth orbit (LEO) satellite networks expanding connectivity and cellular tower operators densifying edge nodes all point to a new hierarchy of value creation. If the next decade truly involves bringing tens of gigawatts of compute online, every link in the chain, from energy procurement to data transmission, becomes essential. This isn't a single-sector story; it's an interlocking system of digital, physical and spatial infrastructure. The lesson for investors is clear: the age of intelligence will be powered not only by algorithms but by the real estate of computation, and the firms building it today will define the industrial landscape of tomorrow.

1 Sources: "Inside the Relentless Race for AI Capacity," Financial Times, August 2025; "OpenAI and NVIDIA Announce Strategic Partnership to Deploy 10 Gigawatts of AI Datacenters," OpenAI, 9/22/25; M. Zeff, "Mark Zuckerberg Says Meta Is Building a 5GW AI Data Center," TechCrunch, 7/14/25.

2 As of 10/13/25, Equinix was a 5.07% weight in WTRE.

3 As of 10/13/25, American Tower was a 4.82% weight in WTRE.

4 As of 10/13/25, Prologis was a 5.80% weight in WTRE.

5 As of 10/13/25, Core Scientific was a 1.39% weight in WTRE.

6 As of 10/13/25, Applied Digital was a 2.22% weight in WTRE.

7 As of 10/13/25, Cipher Mining was a 1.45% weight in WTRE.

8 As of 10/13/25, IREN was a 3.61% weight in WTRE.

9 As of 10/13/25, AST SpaceMobile was a 5.05% weight in WTRE.

10 Source: IREN, "IREN Business Update: 1.4 GW Data Center Project Highlighted," GlobeNewswire, 10/16/24.

11 Sources: Applied Digital Corporation, "Applied Digital Announces Long-Term Supply Agreement with TerraForm Power," GlobeNewswire, 12/19/23; Applied Digital Corporation, "Applied Digital Announces Energization of its 200-Megawatt Datacenter in Garden City, Texas," GlobeNewswire, 10/20/23.

12 Source: AST SpaceMobile, "AST SpaceMobile Announces Definitive Commercial Agreement with Verizon to Support Space-Based Cellular Broadband Across the Continental United States," Business Wire, 10/8/25.

13 Source: "Real Estate Funds," Morningstar.

14 Source: "North America Data Center Trends H1 2025: AI & Hyperscaler Demand Lead to Record-Low Vacancy," CBRE Research, 8/19/25.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Investments in real estate involve additional special risks, such as credit risk, interest rate fluctuations and the effect of varied economic conditions. A Fund focusing on a single country, sector and/or emphasizing investments in smaller companies may experience greater price volatility. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

data.title: Williams-Sonoma: Healthy Comps And Boosting Margins Despite Tariffs
data.description:
To me, one of the most important things we can do as we wrap up 2025 is to drive a substantial rotation in our portfolios. I'm specifically looking to increase my allocation to beaten-down consumer discretionary and enterprise software stocks as well as small- and mid-cap growth names, while locking in gains on the large-cap growth/tech stocks that have driven much of the S&P 500's gains this year.

Investing in retail is tricky this year, since consumer spending has dampened results at many brands that investors once thought invincible. Yet I think there are many "diamonds in the rough" that are offering strong performance at a reasonable price, and Williams-Sonoma (WSM) is one of these companies. Roughly flat since the start of 2025, Williams-Sonoma is achieving mid-single-digit comps growth and has barely seen any profit dent from heavy furniture tariffs.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Williams-Sonoma in September, when the stock was trading higher at just shy of $200 per share. Since then, the company has posted very strong Q3 results, raising its operating margin guidance for the year despite the impact of tariffs. Though we note that the federal government has announced new tariffs on furniture kicking off on Jan. 1, we think Williams-Sonoma's diversified supply chain and its already-demonstrated evidence of working with suppliers to mitigate costs give the company a great edge, and I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this stock.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here are what I consider to be the core tenets of the bull thesis for Williams-Sonoma:

  • Large collection of brands to cover a wide range of customers at multiple price points. Though the company name is Williams-Sonoma, the titular brand is actually less than 20% of sales. Its largest brand, at roughly half of overall sales, is Pottery Barn, with the company also owning a number of smaller labels, including West Elm, Rejuvenation, Mark & Graham, and GreenRow.
  • Strong comp sales and the potential for housing market rebound. Williams-Sonoma achieved a slight acceleration in comp sales this year, which runs counter to the fears that many have about retail stocks this year as consumer spending wanes. We think as well that deciding interest rates and the potential for home buyers to move off the sidelines next year will be another major catalyst for a rebound in the furniture category next year.
  • Excellent tariff management. Despite the heightened tariff burden, Williams-Sonoma has slightly boosted its already-rich gross margin profile (to the high 40%s). We note that the company has a substantially richer gross margin than lower-priced peers like Wayfair (W), which has margins in the ~30% range: giving Williams-Sonoma the ability to compete more on price if it needed to. The company is working with suppliers to take on some of the tariff burden, while the company's own "selective" price increases haven't seemed to dampen demand.
  • Clean balance sheet. Williams-Sonoma has ~$0.9 billion of cash on its balance sheet, which is completely unencumbered of debt, on top of ongoing free cash flow—again, this is an anomaly for a heavy consumer products manufacturer.

Despite these strengths, Williams-Sonoma continues to trade at great value, thanks to investors' fear of investing in consumer-facing stocks. Currently, Wall Street is anticipating the company to hit $8.14 billion in revenue next year (+4% y/y) and $9.15 in pro forma EPS (+5% y/y), roughly in line with the company's current growth trends.

At current share prices near $190, this positions Williams-Sonoma's stock at a 20.8x FY26 P/E ratio. However, when we consider the fact that Williams-Sonoma's $985.8 million of cash is a significant chunk of the company's current ~$22.8 billion market cap, the stock's ex-cash P/E is even lower at ~19.9x FY26 P/E. Though not exactly a bargain-basement stock, Williams-Sonoma is still the value stock of choice in the furniture sector, trading slightly cheaper than Restoration Hardware (RH) and sitting at half of Wayfair's valuation.

We note that this is even though Wayfair is achieving similar mid-single-digit growth rates and has a gross margin deficit to Williams-Sonoma. Take advantage of the discount and recent dip here as a buying opportunity.

Q3 recap

Let's now go through Williams-Sonoma's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Williams-Sonoma's revenue grew 4.6% y/y to $1.88 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.86 billion (+3.6% y/y) by a one-point margin.

All eyes, meanwhile, are on the company's comp sales growth, which, as a reminder, measures the sales growth from existing store locations, excluding the impact of new openings and recent closings. In Williams-Sonoma's case, the company has actually been slightly shrinking its store portfolio, down ~2% y/y to 513 total locations by the end of Q3.

But on the flip side, comp brand sales are accelerating slightly. The chart below showcases comp sales by brand, up 4.0% y/y and accelerating 30bps sequentially from 3.7% growth in Q2. The company saw substantial strength in the core Williams-Sonoma brand, leading the pack at 7.3% comps growth. It's clear here that the company is focused on shrinking its portfolio down to the highest-performing stores, as the total number of Williams-Sonoma brand stores declined 4% y/y to 153 stores, with one net-new closing in Q3 itself. We note as well that the Pottery Barn Kids and Teen label also saw healthy 4.4% y/y growth, helping to partially offset flattish performance at the parent Pottery Barn label.

Management notes that its decision to focus on a heightened in-store experience has translated to higher retail sales and driven market share gains. Per CEO Laura Alber's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

We drove this improvement in performance despite continued geopolitical uncertainty and no substantive improvement in the housing market.

And we continue to gain market share and outperform the industry, which declined again in Q3. Our continued strong results reflect the power of our operating model, industry-leading channel experiences and strong portfolio of brands.

We continue to see exceptional performance in our retail channel, which ran a positive 8.5 comp in Q3. Retail continues to benefit from an improved in-store experience with more inventory availability, enhanced design services and events and the opening of 14 beautiful newly remodeled or repositioned stores so far this year with 7 more to come in Q4.

These are all excellent indicators ahead of the critical holiday season, especially as the company rolls out a new line of gifting products at its youngest brand label, GreenRow.

On the profitability front, Williams-Sonoma also boosted gross margins by 70bps y/y to 46.1%. To me, this is a clear indication that the company has a solid handle on tariffs. As a reminder, the company has a six-point plan for mitigating tariffs, which involves seeking supplier concessions on pricing, changing suppliers to emphasize countries with lower tariff rates, boosting U.S. production, achieving non-tariff supply chain efficiencies as well as cutting down on G&A spending, alongside selective price increases to consumers.

Operating income, meanwhile, grew 5% y/y to $319.1 million, with operating margins of 17.0% expanding 10bps y/y. The company notes that it has rolled out agentic AI customer support across all of its brands, with opportunities for further operating leverage ahead. Pro forma EPS of $1.96 grew 5% y/y and beat Street expectations of $1.87 with 5% upside.

Key takeaways

Not only is Williams-Sonoma one of the cheaper stocks in the furniture sector, but the company is also benefiting from market share gains, accelerating crop sales, and potential tailwinds if lower interest rates drive a rebound in home sales next year. Stay long here and use the near-term dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: GlobalFoundries Sets Its Sights On Physical AI
data.description:
GlobalFoundries Inc. (GFS) is setting the tone for future growth across next-generation markets, with management setting its sights on optical networking and custom silicon for data center AI and physical AI for industrial robotics and automation. With the convergence of AI and robotics, GlobalFoundries’ technology stack is positioned to offer a variety of chips to service a growing proportion of the market, positioning the firm for long-term, durable growth. In addition to this, GlobalFoundries’ global footprint provides substantial flexibility to service customers on a local-for-local basis, shortening the supply chains to service regional demand. Given the robust outlook, I am reiterating my Buy rating for GFS shares with a price target of $48.84/share at 8.64x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

GlobalFoundries Operations

GlobalFoundries’ portfolio may be optimized to cater to the growing power and networking demand as part of the AI data center megatrend. For example, GlobalFoundries’ Bulk CMOS platforms integrate high voltage, embedded memory, and radio frequency (RF) into one platform, providing critical, modular architecture for power-efficient modules. GlobalFoundries’ FDX platform can also be utilized for low-power, high-performance applications in industrial IoT, like automation systems, as well as consumer electronics.

In August 2025, GlobalFoundries completed the acquisition of MIPS, enhancing GlobalFoundries’ AI and processor IP for energy-efficient, high-performance edge computing capabilities. The growth factor management is taking into consideration with the acquisition is the convergence of robotics and AI, given MIPS’s capabilities for real-time performance for autonomous driving, smart devices, and industrial automation. The combined portfolio of low-power AI cores, mixed-signal analog, and real-time communications backlinks creates an appealing portfolio for the next generation of autonomous technologies.

GlobalFoundries has realized a number of design wins across next-generation platforms year-to-date in 2025, including smart glasses, satellite communications, autonomous vehicle smart sensors, and AI-enabled smart medical devices, among others, suggesting strong tailwinds for connected smart devices across multiple industries. In total, GlobalFoundries has gained 400 design wins year-to-date, many of which will begin to positively impact revenue in eFY27. One factor that may drive growth in the physical AI space is the need for more custom silicon designs to enable automation and robotics, a feature that may be enabled through MIPS.

In Q3 ’25, GlobalFoundries outlined its improving positioning in the optical market to service AI data centers, developing pluggable silicon photonics and co-packaged optics as an alternative for copper interconnects. In the quarter, GlobalFoundries won 3 optical networking designs with new customers for an aggregate deal value of $150mm as part of the initial iteration. Long-term, management is expecting pluggable optical transceivers and co-packaged optics to ramp up in 2027, reaching a $1b run rate by the end of the decade.

Satellite communications may be a large growth opportunity for GlobalFoundries over the coming years as more satellite networks are built out to support both commercial and defense applications. In defense, the U.S. is investing in modernizing satcom capabilities to integrate transportable tactical command comms, super high-frequency terminals, anti-jam tactical satcom, and combat service support satellite communications. Though the budget request for satcom for 2026 is softer than 2025 at $114.8mm, the total defense budget involves broad technology modernization to enable connectivity across unmanned aircraft. On the commercial side, satellite launches are expected to continue to grow in support of direct-to-device communications as well as two-way communications for IoT.

GlobalFoundries Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q4 ’25, I’m forecasting GlobalFoundries to generate $1,826mm in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.49/share. My forecast includes incremental margin improvements to operations as revenue scales to dilute costs. Looking further out, I’m forecasting GlobalFoundries to continue to scale as new capacity comes online, particularly to support local for local production in the U.S. and Europe as part of its technology partnerships. One of the core capabilities GlobalFoundries provides is its global foundry footprint, allowing for localized production that may be beneficial to supply chain-focused customers.

GlobalFoundries closed Q3’25 with $3.3b in cash & equivalents and marketable securities and $1.17b in debt on the balance sheet for a net cash position of $2.1b.

Risks Related to GlobalFoundries

Bull Case

GlobalFoundries is building out its capabilities to service the next generation of physical AI, particularly with its recent acquisition of MIPS. With growing demand for local-for-local fabrication, GlobalFoundries’s global footprint is capable of servicing localized demand in the U.S., EU, and China.

Bear Case

Physical AI remains years out and may not mature as planned. Competition for GaN remains intense, with China commanding a large proportion of the market share. Though the automotive market has largely grown with respect to content per vehicle, the sharp decline in electric vehicle production in the U.S. may add pressure to the total content per vehicle in the market.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

GFS shares are currently trading at 7.68x EV/aEBITDA, an appealing premium given shares’ historical trading range. Though shares are currently trading near their 1-year midpoint, I believe shares exhibit substantial room for growth over the coming quarters, particularly as GlobalFoundries picks up design wins for industrial automation and robotics. Given the stable outlook for margin-accretive growth, I am reiterating my Buy rating for GFS shares with a price target of $48.84/share at 8.64x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: BlackSky Technology: Sell The Latest Unwarranted Rally
data.description:
BlackSky Technology (BKSY) has had one of the widest gaps between expectations and underlying fundamentals of any company that I've covered in 2025.

Over and over, traders have bid up on BKSY stock on hopes that the company's business model is finally ready to take flight. And then, seemingly with each subsequent quarterly earnings report, the stock plummets once again as the company whiffs on expectations.

I've called "Sell" on multiple BlackSky Technology rallies this year, such as in an article from July where I had a negative view of the company's preliminary Q2 results:

That came following an earlier rally back in January, which abruptly failed once the firm announced an earnings miss. And then, once again, BlackSky stock ran up in September before shocking investors with another disastrous set of earnings results in the most recent quarter.

Yet, despite having bought into numerous questionable BKSY stock rallies in the past, traders are now bidding up BlackSky shares into year-end. The latest jump came on Monday, with BKSY stock surging as much as 18% on the back of a Jefferies initiation report with a "Buy" rating.

The Bullish Argument

In its initiation report, the Jefferies analyst team wrote that:

"After a modest breather in 2025(sales +6%) on US Gov/EOCL slowness, BlackSky appears well positioned to expand the topline at ~25% per yr based on Gen-3 sat leverage and a robust international customer base".

Using this set of assumptions, Jefferies settled on a $23 price target and "Buy" rating for the stock.

However, after Monday's rally, BKSY stock is now trading around $22/share, meaning there's only a tiny bit of upside left based on Jefferies price target.

That should be unnerving for potential investors. Even if we assume BlackSky's revenues go from their current trajectory (negative year-over-year in recent quarters) to +25% annualized growth, that's apparently the base case we should be looking for merely to support the current stock price. If BlackSky reports slower growth, let alone continues the recent trend of outright negative revenue prints, the stock would likely end up being dramatically overvalued at today's level.

Putting the Jefferies upgrade aside, the broader bullish case is that BlackSky continues to build its backlog. And it is also preparing further deployment of its Gen-3 satellites, which should lead to improved customer demand. In theory, this should result in higher revenues and operating margins over time.

Why I'm Not Buying The Bullish Case

You can go back to past BlackSky conference calls, here's 2025 Q1 for a great example, and find a bullish tone with the company pointing to strong backlog and the Gen 3 satellites as solid growth catalysts. Back in Q1, BlackSky seemed confident in its $125 million-$142 million full-year revenue guide despite acknowledging uncertainty in budget risks due to rapidly shifting geopolitics. At that time, it was reporting 50% year-over-year backlog growth. Yet, subsequent revenues for the full-year 2025 didn't end up seeing any real improvement despite that build in the backlog.

We're now on course for just $109 million in estimated revenues this year, as per the latest analyst consensus (and I think that's too high, as we'll see in a minute). That $109 million would be a more than 10% miss to the bottom end of the firm's full-year revenue guidance range from Q1.

So, consider me unimpressed at hearing the same bullish arguments around backlog and new satellites today. If this potential actually starts translating into meaningful revenue growth again, that would be more impressive. But until then, count me among the skeptics.

Adding to my cautious stance, somehow, in a year full of bad BlackSky earnings reports, Q3 ended up being the worst yet. In November, the company announced that its revenues had plunged 13% year-over-year to $19.6 million. Analysts had been expecting $28.6 million for the period. That's a monumental miss. On an earnings basis, the company lost 44 cents per share, which also fell short of expectations.

Here are the company's revenues by quarter over the past year:

  • Q4 2024: $30.4 million
  • Q1 2025: $29.5 million
  • Q2 2025: $22.2 million
  • Q3 2025: $19.6 million

Despite the supposed catalysts in the form of BlackSky's strong backlog and newer satellites, the company's revenues have steadily gone down with each and every passing quarter.

Furthermore, the company has only generated $71 million in revenues year-to-date, so I think the full-year 2025 estimates remain too aggressive (such as Jefferies forecasting 6% full-year FY'25 growth). I wouldn't be surprised if BlackSky fails to hit $100 million in total 2025 revenues, which would mean that revenues will have fallen outright for the full year as compared to 2024.

This is a growth story on life support.

BKSY Stock Verdict

Maybe BlackSky will eventually be able to turn its business' potential into actual results. Perhaps the explanation around the slowdown this year being due to timing effects and issues with U.S. government budgeting is correct, and these revenues actually start to come through in 2026. Plus, the company's balance sheet is in fine shape; there won't be a liquidity squeeze anytime soon despite the large operating losses. There is still a viable bull case.

But, let's be honest here. After four quarters of earnings misses out of the past five quarters, there is very little credibility around the bull thesis right now. See this chart of the company's actual EPS versus analyst estimates:

The market should be discounting BlackSky stock accordingly. Take space peer Redwire (RDW), for example. RDW stock imploded this summer following a downbeat string of business developments.

In my view, that makes Redwire a speculative "Buy," as there is still a plausible (if currently somewhat shaky) bull case, and the stock has gotten sharply discounted for its operational missteps. By contrast, BlackSky has released one train wreck earnings report after the next, yet the market seemingly refuses to reprice the stock accordingly.

In fact, BlackSky is now trading at 7x revenues. This is for a company that is struggling to post any top-line growth whatsoever this year while continuing to generate large ongoing losses. That's a demanding valuation.

I get that there is exuberance in the space sector thanks to the potential SpaceX IPO. There will be a bid for a lot of space stocks in the wake of that IPO. Yet, investors in the sector should keep BlackSky's increasingly grim track record in mind before buying the stock simply because it's in the same industry as SpaceX.

Put another way, while SpaceX will likely be a huge and highly successful IPO, its prospects won't matter much to BlackSky's future business outlook or profitability. So far, BlackSky's pattern has been to go into earnings with high expectations and then whiff badly, leading to large corrections. With the stock spiking once again on seemingly little more than a Jefferies "Buy" rating along with some SpaceX excitement, BlackSky is once again flying high and set to get clipped if and when Q4 earnings miss the mark.

If anything, I am considering downgrading BKSY stock to "Strong Sell," as the chasm between investor expectations and the firm's actual results continues to widen.

That said, there is a chunky order backlog here, and it's plausible that much of BlackSky's dramatic revenue shortfall in 2025 was due to issues with U.S. government budgeting decisions. Regardless, investors' patience with this company should be wearing awfully thin. If earnings don't look a lot better soon, BKSY stock is likely to trade in the single digits come this time next year.

data.title: S&P 500: Its Not A Buy Or A Sell, Its A Married Put!
data.description:
With all the air in the room taken up by "where's the S&P 500 going to end the new year," as it typically does in late December, I'm taking a different path. I have already posted my 2026 market outlook. But as they say, predictions are for show. Executing to create returns and avoid big loss along the way? That's for dough.

I've devoted a lot of time during 2025 to identifying straightforward ways to use options around my biggest ETF and stock positions. For my investing group here, I presented an ongoing series of collar positions, some of which I discussed here. Yet as the year continued, I saw less value in collaring as frequently as I had been to start the year.

Why? For the same market conditions and evolution I wrote about in my last article, on the 2-ETF portfolio approach. Too much correlation within the US stock market! That means if I apply the collar method to index ETFs like the most liquid optionable ones, S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq 100 (QQQ), I get similar results.

That worked out quite well from a risk management standpoint in March and April of this year. The market tanked quickly, and the collars did what they were supposed to do. The puts I purchased kept my losses to the level I pre-determined to be my "uncle" point when I bought the stock... in a way that stop orders could not assure me of. Which is why I say that stop orders only stop profits, not losses.

And the covered call options declined in value as they should have. Which allowed me to keep much of the income I brought in upon placing the collar around the stocks and ETFs I bought.

That's all well and good. So why am I doing much less of it now? Because the opportunities look too much alike. If I were solely focused on collars, I might think differently. But at this point, while all collars are not the same, they are too similar in one respect: when the market falls hard, most of them will crash through their put strike prices. Good news/bad news: they cut losses, but they were still losses.

And, as the year went on, S&P 500 volatility really declined. To the point where option premiums were lower in price as a percentage of the underlying holding than they had been. That makes put protection cheaper, but covered call writing brings in a lot less cash.

So heading into 2026, my collar outlook is one of consolidation of effort. And that brings me to the main topic here: how would that consolidation, the simpler, broad market SPY ETF, have worked last year?

And with covered call option premiums still muted, it appears the start of 2026 is less about falling in love with collars. And more about getting married. That is, using a "married put" strategy, whereby I skip the covered calls. Why sacrifice the upside potential of SPY (or anything else I'm placing options around) in exchange for a small up front cash payment? The puts are inexpensive enough.

2025: How a 1-year collar or Married Put Would Have Done

There are an infinite number of examples I could use here. The levers are very flexible. Strike price of the put, of the call, and the expiration date of the options. Sometimes they are the same for me, sometimes I run the covered calls out a few months longer. This is why, when subscribers ask me questions about collaring, when those questions go to "what's the standard way you do this?" there is no standard answer. Not for me, at least.

Because I don't apply put and call options based on Greek statistical calculations as many do. I'm a technician, so I use the charts to determine what is logical to me, with the goal being VERY stringent on the downside.

Let's assume the following:

  • I bought 100 shares of SPY at the close of 2024.
  • I sold covered calls and bought puts on those shares, expiring 12/31/25, a few days from now. I know that during the year I would have done with this situation what I did with many others during the year. I would have closed the collar in early April when the S&P 500 fell nearly 20%, and reset it once things calmed down in May.

But let's assume this was a 12-month deal, no changes allowed, with all settling up next week at year-end. And I'll use round numbers to keep it easier to follow. $580 is the approximate closing price for SPY in 2024, so I'll assume I paid that for 100 shares.

This article aims to present a "typical" collar trade for me, and while there's no typical, I do have a consistent approach when it comes to what I look for. From doing this for so long, I tend to find my comfort zone for the puts is to strike them right around the cost of the underlying, allowing the cost of the puts to be about equal to my risk of loss. So in this case, SPY $580 puts to 12/31/25 is what I'll look for.
