I want about 15-20% upside potential in this situation, since we are going out a year and I prefer not to deal with the risk of getting called. That said, if the SPY flew higher and that was a "risk," I'd welcome it. And probably buy more stock or call options to offset the risk of losing the stock. And, since my projected S&P 500 high point, written in my 2025 outlook article a year ago, was $6,800. So I'll use $680 for the SPY call option strike.

Now, let's look up the numbers and see how it would have done.

Here's the history on that protective put contract.

And here's the covered call.

So in round numbers:

$580 purchase price

Pay $28 for the put protection, and receive $7 for the calls. A $21 net cost.

That made the effective up and down limits equal to $580 and $680, minus $21 on each side. Or, $559 to $659 a SPY share. From $580 purchase price, that's $79 of upside and $21 of downside (my put cost, as noted above).

That's nearly 4:1 upside in dollar terms. I would have jumped at that. And I did, many times during the year, on SPY and other ETFs, as well as several stocks, as my investing group subscribers recall.

Here's a pseudo-snapshot of this collar as of Monday, 12/22/25 close.

I say pseudo because the $680 calls would have been exercised by now, in all likelihood. But I got to offset more than 1/5 the cost of the 12-month put protection. And the $580 puts are essentially worthless, barring an October 19, 1987 repeat between now and New Year's Eve next week.

So a "don't touch it" approach would have essentially knocked a bit off my return for the year versus buy and hold. But as was the case with many collars for me in 2025, March and April were worth whatever upside I didn't get later. Because with the put options in place, I could sleep well every night, knowing I could not do worse than $580. Even when SPY went 100 points lower than that in the chaos of that period.

2026: Married Put, Not Collar, is the Standard

Every situation is a bit different, but consider the VIX here. It's as low as it's been all year, under 15. That's a market not afraid of much.

So, that's a market I want to hedge! But using puts only, as the call premium will not be of good value. Note to covered call ETF fans: this impacts you too, for the same reason.

As they say in pickup basketball, let's run it back. For 2026, that is. Here's the full collar (options out to 12/18/26, as 12/31/26 not available for a few days). You can see that the call premium covers a lot less of the put cost than a year ago. That's due to that low VIX. And it follows that for 5% downside, I get 15% upside, or 3:1 as my up/down ratio. It was 4:1 a year ago. 3:1 is still solid, but why give up the upside? So I won't.

By pursuing this as a married put approach instead of a collar, the SPY position for the next 12 months boils down to this:

  • 5% maximum downside risk from this current level, all year
  • 100% of SPY's upside potential, all year

To each their own, but I'll take that for a portion of my portfolio. Remember, I can always write covered calls later, and try to pay for some of those puts.

Concluding thoughts

I hope this helps some investors better understand both the flexibility and decision-making process involved in using options around ETFs and stocks. I discuss this type of thing regularly with my investing group, and write a lot of collar and married put articles for another publication. So here, as a new year approaches, I wanted to post one semi-timeless discussion of how I approach these. Again, every situation is different, but my goal was to try to respond to the many general questions I get from the broader Seeking Alpha audience.

This is as good a time as I can recall for investors with high equity exposure (appreciated most likely) to understand the basics of protecting it without constantly having to debate "do I sell my stocks?" For now, puts can do the job for me by themselves, in most cases. When volatility spikes again, as it surely will. I'll return to strong consideration of the covered calls, the other side of the collar.
